coredata.dc:title: S-doped carbon dots capped ZnCdTe quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of guanine
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xin
He, Li
Long, Yuwei
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Yang, Jidong
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs)-based ratiometric fluorescence probe have drawn extensive attention in recent years. However, intrinsic dual-emitting QDs were rarely reported, and most of them were based on the metal doped-QDs. Herein, an intrinsic dual-emitting ZnCdTe QDs were synthesized by using fluorescence S doped-carbon dots (CDs) as capping ligands for the first time in this work, two emission peaks (derived from CDs and QDs respectively) were observed in CDs-ZnCdTe QDs and the probe showed excellent stability and optical properties. Moreover, we found that the fluorescence emission of QDs could be quenched by guanine via excited state electron transfer or the aggregation of QDs, but the emission of CDs remained stable, thus we successfully developed a ratiometric probe for the determination of guanine. Under optimized conditions, the detection limit of guanine was 0.076 μmol⋅L−1 (3σ/k), with a linear range from 0.25 to 40.0 μmol⋅L−1. The smart ratiometric fluorescence probe was demonstrated for the detection of guanine in DNA samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 96.95 to 102.50%, indicating it was a promising biosensor for highly selectively and sensitively determination of guanine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Carbon dots
Ligand
Ratiometric fluorescence
Guanine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs and l-cysteine capped ZnCdTe
Feasibility assay of the sensing system
Determination of guanine in DNA samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed mechanism of fluorescence sensing guanine by CDs-ZnCdTe QDs.
Structure of guanine and proposed binding model of QDs-Guanine complex.
(A) TEM image of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (right inset, the corresponding size distribution histogram of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs) and (B) TEM image of S-doped carbon dots.
(A) XRD pattern of the CDs (a) and CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (b). (B) FTIR spectra of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (a) and CDs (b).

(A) Fluorescence emission of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs under different heating time. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of l-Cysteine capped ZnCdTe QDs and CDs-ZnCdTe QDs. (C) The overlapping of emission (a) excitation (b) and absorption (c) spectra of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs.
The effect of pH (A) and ion strength (B) to the as-prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs.

(A) The fluorescence response of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs towards 30.0 μmol⋅L−1 guanine, the inset displays the photographs of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution before (a) and after (b) the addition of guanine under irradiation of UV light. (B) The effects of pH values to the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs + guanine (35.0 μmol⋅L−1) system. (C) The effect of incubation time to the sensing system.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs-Zn;CdTe QDs with different concentrations of guanine. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio of QDs (I 605/I 425) and the concentration of guanine.

The fluorescence response of the CDs–ZnCdTe QDs in the presence of different substances (Guanine 30.0 μ mol⋅L−1, metal ions concentration 3.0 mmol⋅L−1, BSA 10.0 mg⋅ mL-1 and other substance was 1.0 mmol⋅L−1).

(A) The time-resolved decay spectra of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (a) and after the addition of 10.0 μmol⋅L−1 (b) and 30.0 μmol⋅L−1 guanine (c). (B) The UV-vis absorption of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs + 30.0 μmol⋅L−1 guanine (a), guanine (b) and CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (c), the inset displays the photographs of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution before (a) and after (b) the addition of guanine under visible light. (C) The fluorescence spectrums of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs + 30.0 μmol⋅L−1 guanine system before (blue dash line) and after the addition of 50.0 μmol⋅L−1 Cu2+ solution (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Results for the determination of guanine in DNA samples.

head.ce:title: S-doped carbon dots capped ZnCdTe quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of guanine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Chongqing Three Gorges University, Wanzhou, Chongqing 404100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs)-based ratiometric fluorescence probe have drawn extensive attention in recent years. However, intrinsic dual-emitting QDs were rarely reported, and most of them were based on the metal doped-QDs. Herein, an intrinsic dual-emitting ZnCdTe QDs were synthesized by using fluorescence S doped-carbon dots (CDs) as capping ligands for the first time in this work, two emission peaks (derived from CDs and QDs respectively) were observed in CDs-ZnCdTe QDs and the probe showed excellent stability and optical properties. Moreover, we found that the fluorescence emission of QDs could be quenched by guanine via excited state electron transfer or the aggregation of QDs, but the emission of CDs remained stable, thus we successfully developed a ratiometric probe for the determination of guanine. Under optimized conditions, the detection limit of guanine was 0.076 μmol⋅L−1 (3σ/k), with a linear range from 0.25 to 40.0 μmol⋅L−1. The smart ratiometric fluorescence probe was demonstrated for the detection of guanine in DNA samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 96.95 to 102.50%, indicating it was a promising biosensor for highly selectively and sensitively determination of guanine.

ce:section.ce:para: Guanine, one of four essential bases in DNA and RNA, is of great importance to human and other creatures. As previous research reported, guanine is the most vulnerable to oxidize in nuclide acid base, on the basis of guanine is one of final products of nucleic acids degradation metabolism, may lesions are involved with the change of its content in organism [1], such as cancer and AIDs. Obviously, there are no doubts that the determination of guanine in organism becomes a pressing issue, many studies have focused on the detection of guanine so far, these methods are mainly based on electrochemical methods [2–5], chromatography [6,7], and capillary electrophoresis [8,9]. Though these methods own low detection limit, lacking of portable detection environment become a common problem. Therefore, it still remains a problem for developing more convenient, sensitive and stable methods for guanine detection. Fluorescence sensing have always been regarded as a promising method for biosensing owing to simple operation, low cost and high sensitive [10,11]. In recent years, several fluorescent probes have been developed for guanine sensing [12–15], Feng et al. [13]. and Zhang et al. [15] reported a carbon dots based fluorescent probe for guanine determination, both of their methods showed a desirable result, but low tolerance and high background remains a question. However, Ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes could provide two or more signals, where one can act as reference to normalize the analyte-responsed signal, the effects of nonspecific background can be reduced to a minimum [16], which further improved the sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence sensing system. Consequently, developing a more reliable ratiometric fluorescence probe for guanine determination should be received much more attentions.

Since 1980s, the colloidal quantum dots (QDs) have become a star nanomaterial because of the excellent optical and electronic properties [17–19]. In brief, quantum dots are colloidal nanocrystal which made from II/VI and III/V elements, and diameters distribute at the range of 2–10 nm. In comparisons to conventional organic fluorophores, QDs have some attractive optical properties, including spectrally narrow, relatively symmetric, and particle size-dependent and surface-dependent fluorescence emission [20–22]. More importantly, with great development of synthetic approaches over the last decades, QDs could be easily prepared with tunable emission range from UV-blue to the mid infrared by size and surface modification [23–25], which provide researchers more chance to extend the application of QDs. Nowadays, QDs’ applications span many areas range from field-effect transistors, solar cells, memory elements, light emitting diodes, biosensors, bioimaging probes and even catalyst [26–32]. However, many studies pointed out that the heavy metal-containing (e.g., cadmium, mercury.) QDs core could render the toxicity [33–35], so it has became a major limitation of QDs’ application. To our delight, surface modification is an intriguing tool that makes the QDs less toxic [36], so it is definitely a good solution to improve the properties of quantum dots by replacing more biocompatible materials with traditional capping ligands.

Since carbon dots were discovered by Xu et al. [37] during the purification of single walled carbon nanotubes in 2004, it has been a fantastic fluorescence nanomaterials in the last decade, amounts of studies have focused on the synthesis and application of carbon dots because of their remarkable merits, such as strong luminescence, high tolerance to photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility [38,39], thus carbon dots are promising materials in the fields of biosensing, bioimaging and so on [40–43]. What is more, there are abundant surface functional groups in the surface CDs, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and so on [39], so CDs are similar with amino acids in some degrees. It is reported that sulfur doped carbon dots exist sulfydryl [44,45], as we all know that amino acids including –SH are usually used as the capping ligand of QDs, thus it is possible to synthesize QDs by using S doped CDs as capping ligands and reduce the toxicity of QDs to extend the application.

In this work, in order to establish less toxic QDs based ratiometric fluorescence probe, S-doped CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of ammonium citrate and l-cysteine, and then ZnCdTe QDs were firstly synthesized by using CDs as capping ligands. Two emission peak at 420 (intrinsic emission of CDs) and 605 nm (emission of QDs) were observed in the as prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs and following experiments revealed it was pretty stable under some extreme conditions. Moreover, we found guanine could efficiently quench the emission at 605 nm through excited state electron transfer or the aggregation of QDs (Scheme 1 ), hereby, a highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence probe for guanine detection was established.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with a 1 cm path quartz cuvette. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Hitachi U-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The morphology and sizes of CDs and QDs were analyzed with a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA). A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Kyoto, Japan) was used to identify functional groups of the as-prepared CDs and QDS. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was performed with an AXS-D8 X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). A FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) was employed to measure fluorescence lifetime at room temperature. The pH values of solutions were monitored with a PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd., China).

Ammonium citrate, Sodium borohydride and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Potassium tellurite, cadmium acetate, zinc acetate, l-cysteine and guanine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China.). The Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solutions at different pH values were prepared by mixing the mixed acid (composed of 2.71 mL 85% H3PO4, 2.36 mL HAc and 2.47 g H3BO3) with 0.20 mol L−1 NaOH in different proportions. All reagents were analytical grade and were used without further purification, and deionized water was used throughout the whole research.

CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis according to the previous research [46]. In brief, 0.20 g ammonium citrate and 0.10 g l-cysteine were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water to form a transparent solution, and then the solution was transferred to a 250 mL conical flask ultrasonic for 5 min. Then the mixed solution was placed into a domestic microwave oven for 6 min until a pale yellow foam solid was formed at the bottom. The reaction mixture was dissolved with 30 mL deionized water, and then the solution was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rpm min−1) to remove insoluble particles. Finally, the obtained solution was kept at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs and l-cysteine capped ZnCdTe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of QDs was based on previous literatures with slight modification [47]. Firstly, the preparation of precursor solution was described below, 42.64 mg cadmium acetate and 8.78 mg zinc acetate was dissolved in 50.0 mL deionized water in a three-necked flask, and 2.0 mL as prepared CDs solution was added under vigorous stirring, then the pH was adjusted to 11.0 by dropwise adding 1 mol⋅L −1 NaOH solution., 8.86 mg sodium tellurite was added into the above solution after stirring for 5 min, followed by adding 50.0 mL deionized water. Until the sodium tellurite was dissolved, 4.0 mL 0.53 mol⋅L −1 NaBH4 solution was put into the above precursor, then the solution was refluxed at a 105 °C oil bath under open-air conditions. When the solution cooled to room temperature, two times of acetone was added, and then the solution was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rpm⋅ min−1) to obtain the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs after shaking well. The obtained CDs-ZnCdTe QDs was dried at vacuum oven for 2 h and redissolved in 100.0 mL deionized water. The synthesis procedure of l-cysteine capped ZnCdTe was the same with CDs-ZnCdTe QDs except changing the CDs with l-cysteine.

200.0 μL CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution was diluted to 1.80 mL by pH 7.0 BR buffer solutions, and then 200.0 μL different concentrations of guanine solution were added at room temperature. After incubation for 5 min, the solution was placed into a 1 cm path quartz cuvette to measure fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded from 375 from 700 nm, and fluorescence intensities at 425 and 605 nm were used for determination of guanine, all results were obtained at least in triplicate.

The TEM image of CDs and CDs-ZnCdTe QDs was shown in Fig. 1 , it revealed that the CDs and as prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs both possessed a sphere-like shape. And both of them were densely and uniformly dispersed in aqueous with a narrow size distribution (CDs-ZnCdTe QDs 3.50 ± 0.93 nm, CDs 2.0 ± 0.20 nm). The size distribution indicated that S-doped carbon dots were smaller than CDs-ZnCdTe QDs, therefore, the size of S-doped CDs satisfied the requirement of size distribution of capping ligand of quantum dots.

In order to verify the crystalline structures of CDs and QDs, the phase constitution of the nanometerials were studied by XRD patterns (Fig. 2 A). The diffraction of CDs (black line) was sharp at 32°, and no impurity peaks were observed, indicating their highly crystalline nature and the high purity of CDs. The peaks around 10°, 22° and 35° showed the highly crystalline nature of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (red line), meanwhile, CDs-ZnCdTe QDs had the same diffraction peak at around 32°, which showed the CDs were successfully attached to the QDs core further. As depicted in Fig. 2B, the FTIR spectrum of CDs and as prepared QDs, the FTIR absorption bands of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs were majorly located at 1049.9 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CO), 1398.0 cm−1 (stretching vibration of COO−), 1569.0 cm−1 (stretching vibration of NH2), 2928.0 cm−1 (stretching vibration of CH), and 3424.9 cm−1 (stretching vibration of OH). The FTIR absorption bands of CDs were almost the same as CDs-ZnCdTe QDs except the stretching vibration of SH (2569.2 cm−1), the appearance of carboxyl and –SH groups in S-doped CDs and the disappearance of –SH in CDs-ZnCdTe QDs indicated that the surface of QDs were covered by CDs.

Fig. 3 A demonstrated the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs under different reflux time. With the prolonging of reaction time, the emission peak of QDs shifted to longer wavelength and the corresponding fluorescence intensity also increased. The emission peak at 425 nm which could be seen as the emission of CDs was almost invariant, indicating that the optical properties of CDs were not influenced after bounding to the QDs core, therefore, it is rational to design a CDs capped ZnCdTe QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe. Impressively, we found that the red shift of QDs emission peak was faster than previously reported method [47], the emission peak shifted from 555 nm to 605 nm during the refluxing of 70 min. When S-doped CDs was replaced by the l-cysteine and the reaction time was the same as the synthesis of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs, the emission peak appeared at 510 nm (Fig. 3B) and the average diameter of l-cysteine capped ZnCdTe QDs was calculated to 2.60 nm by Peng’s method [48]. Therefore, it can be concluded that when S-doped CDs was attached to the surface of QDs, its intrinsic diameter make QDs size became larger lead to the large red shift of QDs emission. And it is possible to synthesize the near-infrared QDs-based probe under shorter time by using CDs as the capping ligand, unfortunately, the two emissions both decrease intensively with the prolonging of reflux time in our experiment, so 70 min was chosen as the proper reaction time. Fig. 3C showed the fluorescence emission, excitation spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs. The fluorescence emission peaks at 425 nm and 605 nm (black line) could be clearly observed, and the full-width at half-maximum (fwhm) of the fluorescence spectra was narrow (∼50 nm), indicating the as-prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs were homogenous and monodisperse. And an interesting phenomena was observed during the experiment, we firstly prepared the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs with the purified CDs solution (purified by dialysis 1000 Da MWCO dialysis bag), the emission of QDs was pretty low, but the emission intensity of QDs was increased sharply when we used unpurified CDs. We assumed that the very little residual l-cysteine could as an initiator in the formation of QDs core, but we didn’t get enough direct evidences about our assumption, so we would concentrate on this phenomenon in our next study. In order to avoid the interference of other raw materials, we purified our as-prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs by adding two times of acetone and then centrifuged to get the pure CDs-ZnCdTe QDs for further use. The UV–vis absorption peaks of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs appeared at 220 and 560 nm, the peak at 220 nm may be associated with the SH and the π–π* transition of CC bonds corresponding to CDs [47,49], and the absorption at 560 nm (top right of Fig. 3C) was consistent with the previous research [47], demonstrating the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs were successfully synthesized.

Stability is an important indicator for evaluating the quality of probe, so the effect of pH and ion strength towards the fluorescence emission of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at the 605 nm was strongly influenced when the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution was under an acid (1.0–6.0) conditions, but the signal increased and then remained stable in an alkaline environment (7.0–12.0). The phenomena may attribute to the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl on the surface of the CDs, it is reported that carboxyl could act as non-radiative electron–hole recombination centers [38], but the process was strongly prohibited after deprotonation of carboxyl, thus the fluorescence of QDs increased. And as shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio defined as I 605/I 425 was observed rather stable under extremely high ion strength solutions (1.0 mol⋅L−1). The above results indicated the as-prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs was a promising ratiometric fluorescence probe in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility assay of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the response of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs toward guanine, we firstly examined the fluorescence change of the probe after the addition of 30.0 μmol⋅L−1 guanine solution. As shown in Fig. 5 A, though the emission peak at 605 nm decreased sharply after the addition of guanine, the signal derived from CDs almost remained stable. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing for guanine detection were established with dual-emitting CDs-ZnCdTe QDs. Contrast to the most reference-containing ratiometric fluorescence probes which were established by physical incorporation of two different nanoprobes, the reference signal was introduced by covalent bonding. Therefore, the as prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs was rather stable than traditional reference-containing fluorescence probe, and our method exhibited advantages for eliminating the error attached to variations of probe concentration by the intrinsic dual signal of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs.

The solution pH played a vital role in the sensing system, thus the effect of pH to the sensing system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5B, when the pH was high than 9.0, the quench efficiency of guanine decreased significantly, and when the pH ranged from 6.0 to 9.0, the quench efficiency of guanine was almost the same. But the fluorescence intensity difference between 605 nm and 425 nm was the highest when the pH was 7.0, therefore pH 7.0 BR buffer was chosen for the subsequent guanine detection. We assumed that guanine was prone to bind with carboxyl rather than the QDs core due to the deprotonation of the carboxyl under alkaline conditions, so the quench efficiency was lower than acid conditions. The incubation time was also investigated, as Fig. 5C demonstrated that fast response of the CDs-ZnCdTe QDs to the guanine within 6 min, and the I605/I425 value also kept unchangeable after 30 min, indicating that our method not only simplified the detection process, and also possessed the reliability at the same time. Therefore, 5 min was chosen as the incubation time for the following experiments of guanine detection.

Under Optimized condition, fluorescence spectrums were recorded after different concentrations of guanine were incubated in CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of 605 nm gradually decreased with the increasing of the guanine concentration. And Fig. 6B displayed the good linear correlation (R2 = 0.995) between guanine concentration and I 605/I 425 at the range of 0.25–40.0 μmol⋅L −1 with a detection limit of 76.0 nmol⋅L −1 based on the 3σ/k rule, the calibration curve was expressed as I 605 /I 425 = −0.372c + 14.97 [c: concentration of guanine (μmol⋅L −1)]. The responses of l-cysteine capped ZnCdTe QDs to different concentration of guanine were also recorded, the fluorescence intensity of l-cysteine ZnCdTe QDs was nearly unchangeable after the addition of low concentration guanine solution, it showed unsatisfied linear range between 20.0 and 70.0 μmol⋅L −1 (linear correlation R2 = 0.972), and detection limit was determined to 3.6 μmol⋅L −1. These results demonstrated that CDs-ZnCdTe QDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing system was suitable for highly sensitive detection of guanine and hold great potential to apply in practical samples.

Highly selectivity is of great importance to a newly established probe, it involves the applications in practical complex samples, because the activity of target might be influenced by other co-existing foreign substances in samples. Therefore, we investigated the fluorescence response of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs towards other competing substances, such as metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+), protein (BSA, Trypsin), amino acid (Serine, Glycine, Glutathione), purine (8-Mercaptopurine, Adenine, Xanthine), pyrimidine (Thymine, Cytosine) and so on. As Fig. 7 shown, a minimal decrease of the I 605/I 425 value were observed after the addition of other competing substances, indicating that the established ratiometric fluorescence probe exhibited high selectivity for guanine detection.

We deduced that the excited-state electron transfer and the aggregation of QDs lead to the fluorescence quenching of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (Scheme 1). It is reported that guanine are the most sensitive base to oxidation in DNA, thus electron transfer reactions are prone to occur [50]. Taking into consideration of guanine could be regard as an excellent electron donor in CDs-ZnCdTe QDs-guanine system, we speculated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs may be realized through excitation-state electron transfer. Guanine contains two known active sites for cation coordination which is N7 and O6 atom, respectively, and as previously research reported that the interaction energies ΔE HF of guanine to Cd2+, Zn2+ is -199.0 and -248.7 respectively (ΔE HF are the interaction energies evaluated at Hartree-Fock (HF) theoretical level using the function counterpoise method) [51], indicating that the coordination between guanine and Cd, Zn are pretty stable. Therefore, guanine-QDs complex (as Scheme 2 shown) were easily formed through the coordination between Cd, Zn and N7, O6 atoms in the guanine-CDs-ZnCdTe QDs system, therefore the guanine could be absorbed to the surface of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs, leading to the distance between QDs and guanine satisfied the conditions of excited state electron transfer [52].

Interestingly, we found that the colour of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs solution changed to dark brown after the addition of high concentration guanine and the emission peak of QDs shifted to longer wavelength, we assumed that the above phenomena maybe due to the aggregation of QDs. Obviously, the absorption band of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs with guanine (red line) was not simply superposition of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs (black line) and guanine (blue line) as Fig. 8B shown, and the absorption band at 560 nm of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs also disappeared, indicating that the structures of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs may be destroyed after the addition of guanine. Then we employed Cu2+ to prove our assumption, there was no obvious fluorescence recovery of CDs-ZnCdTe QDs after addition of Cu2+ ions in our experiments (Fig. 8C). Therefore, we assumed that the red shift and signal decrease of the emission peak of QDs may be caused by the aggregation of QDs, because larger size quantum dots created a decrease in energy band gap and emitted large wavelength photons (red-shift) [21]. In brief, guanine could compete with CDs to occupy the surface of QDs, what is more, guanine-CDs-ZnCdTe QDs complex could link to each other through three intermolecular H bonds [55] in a high guanine solution, leading to the aggregation of QDs and the fluorescence of QDs was quenched.

In summary, the fluorescence of QDs was quenched efficiently by guanine, and the quenching mechanism was owing to the excited states electron transfer and the aggregation of QDs. In details, excited states electron transfer played the major role in the fluorescence quenching of QDs under a low guanine concentration, because there was no obvious red shift of QDs emission peak. But with the increasing of guanine concentration, the aggregation of QDs induced by the three intermolecular H bonds of guanine-QDs complex was the major origin of quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of guanine in DNA samples
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-ZnCdTe QDs were then used in calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) and herring sperm DNA (hsDNA) samples to monitor the guanine concentrations. DNA samples were prepared according to previous research [8]. In brief, 3.0 mg ctDNA and hsDNA were digested by using 1.0 mL HCl solution (1.0 mol⋅L−1) in a 10.0 mL sealed tube respectively, and then the above solutions were placed in a boiling water bath for 2 h. When the solutions were cooled to room temperature, the pH of the solutions were adjusted to neutral with 1.0 mol⋅L−1 NaOH, then the solutions was diluted to 10.0 mL using pH 7.0 BR buffer solutions. The guanine concentration of DNA samples were determined by standard addition method, the results were listed in Table 1 . It could be seen that the recoveries were ranged from 96.95 to 100.57%, with the RSD of 1.41–3.54%. The above results revealed that the prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs could be applied to practical samples with highly sensitivity and reproducibility.

In summary, we have developed a novel ratiometric probe for the detection of guanine based on the excited state electron transfer and the aggregation of the probe. It was the first time that CDs were regarded as capping ligands to synthesize the CdTe QDs, the as prepared CDs-ZnCdTe QDs show the good stability even under extreme ion strength, and it concluded two emissions at 425 and 605 nm because of the intrinsic emission of CDs, however, the role of very little l-cysteine remaining in unpurified CDs in the formation of QD requires thorough research. Impressively, CDs-ZnCdTe QDs exhibited highly sensitivity and selectivity toward determination of guanine, the fluorescence emission of QDs were efficiently quenched by guanine, thus a ratiometric fluorescence probe for guanine detection were established. Furthermore, the probe showed a reliable result for guanine sensing in DNA sample, we believed that the dual-emitting CDs-ZnCdTe QDs will play a significant role in detection of guanine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21705132 and 21475014) and the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin Xu, A second-year graduate student of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China.
Li He, An undergraduate student of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China.
Yuwei, Long. An undergraduate student of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing.
Shuang, Pan, A lecturer of School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China.
Hui Liu, Associate professor of College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China. 400715, Chongqing, China.
Jidong Yang, Professor of Analytical Chemistry School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China.
Xiaoli Hu, Professor of Analytical Chemistry School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Southwest University, 400715, Chongqing, China.
